findings.description: search() fetches the entire non-deleted users table via fetchall() with no LIMIT, then filters/sorts/paginates in Python, so cost and memory scale O(N) with the whole table to return one page_size window instead of pushing ORDER BY/LIMIT/OFFSET into SQL.
Every fetched row is materialized into a Python dict before any pagination, so a search on a large table builds N dicts of which all but ~page_size are immediately discarded.

name_pattern filtering runs matcher.search() in a Python list comprehension across the full result set (N interpreter-level regex calls) instead of using SQL LIKE/GLOB/REGEXP that could leverage an index for prefix patterns.
The full result set is sorted in Python (users.sort) before slicing the page; the ORDER BY belongs in SQL where an index can satisfy it, avoiding an O(N log N) in-process sort of the entire table.

_connect() opens a brand-new sqlite3 connection on every public-method call with no pooling/reuse, giving a cold page cache per request and per-call connection setup on the hot path under web-service load.

offset = page * page_size with documented 1-based paging skips the first page entirely (page 1 returns rows 21-40); correct offset is (page - 1) * page_size — deferred to critic-logic, flagged here as it governs the page slice.

departments() runs SELECT DISTINCT ... ORDER BY over the full users table on every call; the small, slow-changing department list is a strong candidate for caching instead of repeatedly full-scanning users.

User-supplied name_pattern is compiled and executed as a regex over per-row data, enabling catastrophic-backtracking ReDoS (e.g. (a+)+$) — complexity-as-DoS via untrusted input, deferred to critic-security.
